coredata.dc:title: Nanoreactor-confined synthesis and separation of yellow-luminescent graphene quantum dots with a recyclable SBA-15 template and their application for Fe(III) sensing
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2015 Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Xu, Hongbo
Zhou, Shenghai
Xiao, Lili
Li, Shouzhu
Song, Ting
Wang, Yi
Yuan, Qunhui
coredata.dc:description: Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) have inspired growing research efforts. Although continuous progresses have been achieved in the syntheses of diverse GQDs, it is still a challenge in size-controllable and high-yield preparation of well dispersed GQDs via simple routes. Herein, we present a top-down synthesis of GQDs by using a SBA-15 template as a nanoreactor via a HNO3 vapor cutting strategy. With the recyclable ordered mesoporous SBA-15 template and a silica grid, a high-yield (48%) preparation as well as a facile separation of yellow-luminescent GQDs have been achieved. The obtained GQDs containing abundant oxygen-containing groups (ca. 30% for oxygen content) showed a narrow size distribution (2.5–5.2nm), good water solubility, good photostability and high selectivity for Fe(III) sensing in tap water with satisfying recovery. Further experiments indicated that Cu(II), Co(II), Mn(II), Ni(II) could also quench the fluorescence of the as-prepared GQD after its surface states being adjusted, which might presage more possible applications of this material.

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of ordered mesoporous silica template (SBA-15)
Structure and morphology characterization of SBA-15 and C/SBA-15
Structure and morphology characterization of GQDs
Optical properties and sensing performance of the GQDs
UV–vis absorption and fluorescence property of the GQDs

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Natural Science Foundation of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors acknowledge the financial support from the ‘One Hundred Talents Project Foundation Program’, the ‘Cross-Cooperation Program for Creative Research Teams’ and the ‘Western Light Program’ from ‘ Chinese Academy of Sciences ’ and ‘ National Natural Science Foundation of China ’ ( 21203244 , 21473247 ), the ‘ Young Creative Sci-Tech Talents Cultivation Project of Xinjiang Uyghur Autonomous Region ( 2013711012 )’ as well as the ‘ Xinjiang International Science and Technology Cooperation Project ( 20146003 )’. Appendix A

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic diagram of the preparation of GQDs. (A color version of this figure can be viewed online.)

XRD patterns of the original SBA-15 (A), its carbon replica C/SBA-15 (B) and the regenerated SBA-15 after usage (D). (C) Schematic diagram representing the regeneration of SBA-15 template. (A color version of this figure can be viewed online.)

Nitrogen adsorption isotherms as well as the size distributions of SBA-15 template (A and C) and its carbon replica C/SBA-15 (B and D), representing the ordered mesoporous structure of the C/SBA-15 composite that derived from the SBA-15 template. (A color version of this figure can be viewed online.)

SEM and TEM images of SBA-15 template (A and C) and its carbon replica C/SBA-15 (B and D), representing the ordered mesoporous structure of the C/SBA-15 composite that derived from the SBA-15 template.

Typical TEM and AFM images of the GQDs (A and B). Size distribution and height profile of the GQDs (C and D), indicating the formation of the graphene-layered GQDs with narrow size distribution. (A color version of this figure can be viewed online.)

FT-IR and XPS spectra of the GQDs (A and B), representing the existence of rich oxygen-containing groups on the surface of GQDs. High-resolution XPS C1s (C) and O1s (D) spectra of the GQDs. (A color version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(A) UV–vis absorption spectrum of the GQDs solution. Inset: photographs of the GQDs solution excited by daylight (left) and UV lamp with 365nm (right). (B) PL emission spectra of the GQDs aqueous solution at different excitation wavelengths from 310 to 430nm. (A color version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(A) PL emission spectra of the GQDs solution in the presence of different concentrations of Fe(III) (from top to bottom: 0, 3, 5, 10, 20, 30, 40, 50 and 60μM). (B) Calibration curve for Fe(III) detection. I 0 and I are the normalized PL intensities at λ ex =370nm of the GQDs in the absence and presence of metal ions, respectively. (C) Quenching performance of the GQDs solution in the presence of various metal ions at the same concentration of 80μM (from left to right: Ag(I), Al(III), Ca(II), Cd(II), Co(II), Fe(III), Cu(II), Pb(II), Mg(II), Mn(II), Hg(II), K(I), Na(I), Zn(II) and Ni(II)). (D) Time-dependent photoluminescence spectrum of the GQDs solution under 370nm excitation at room temperature (Xe lamp, 150W). (A color version of this figure can be viewed online.)

head.ce:title: Nanoreactor-confined synthesis and separation of yellow-luminescent graphene quantum dots with a recyclable SBA-15 template and their application for Fe(III) sensing

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Laboratory of Environmental Science and Technology, Xinjiang Technical Institute of Physics & Chemistry, Key Laboratory of Functional Materials and Devices for Special Environments, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Urumqi 830011, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Laboratory of Environmental Science and Technology
Xinjiang Technical Institute of Physics & Chemistry
Key Laboratory of Functional Materials and Devices for Special Environments

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: University of Chinese Academy of Sciences, Beijing 100049, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: University of Chinese Academy of Sciences

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) have inspired growing research efforts. Although continuous progresses have been achieved in the syntheses of diverse GQDs, it is still a challenge in size-controllable and high-yield preparation of well dispersed GQDs via simple routes. Herein, we present a top-down synthesis of GQDs by using a SBA-15 template as a nanoreactor via a HNO3 vapor cutting strategy. With the recyclable ordered mesoporous SBA-15 template and a silica grid, a high-yield (48%) preparation as well as a facile separation of yellow-luminescent GQDs have been achieved. The obtained GQDs containing abundant oxygen-containing groups (ca. 30% for oxygen content) showed a narrow size distribution (2.5–5.2nm), good water solubility, good photostability and high selectivity for Fe(III) sensing in tap water with satisfying recovery. Further experiments indicated that Cu(II), Co(II), Mn(II), Ni(II) could also quench the fluorescence of the as-prepared GQD after its surface states being adjusted, which might presage more possible applications of this material.

ce:section.ce:para: Graphene quantum dots (GQDs), as a novel kind of photoluminescent (PL) carbon dots (CDs) that containing graphene nanosheets with lateral dimensions less than 100nm in single, double or few (3–10) layers, have attracted tremendous attention [1,2]. They are superior in terms of chemical inertness, low toxicity, high biocompatibility and excellent photostability compared to organic dyes and semiconductor quantum dots [3]. Moreover, due to the disadvantages of size effects that possessed by common CDs [4], their cousin GQDs have attracted growing interest because of the excellent physical and chemical properties, such as high surface area, larger length-to-diameter ratio (the ratio of length to thickness), better surface grafting via the π–π conjugated network because of their structural graphene layers [5]. These unique properties enable the promising applications of GQDs in sensors [6], supercapacitors [7], photovoltaics [8], drug delivery and bioimaging [9], etc.

Recent progresses have shown that great efforts have been made to fulfill the preparation of GQDs [1,6–9]. The reported synthetic methods can be summed up to top-down and bottom-up strategies [2,10]. The top-down strategies refer to cut bulk carbon materials into GQDs via approaches such as electrochemical oxidation [11], electron beamlithography [12], acidic oxidation [13], hydrothermal [14] and solvothermal treatments [15]. While the bottom-up routes mainly depend on solution chemistry [16], cage-opening of fullerene [17] and carbonization of organic precursors [18]. Both routes have their own advantages in preparation of the GQDs except for some non-ignorable disadvantages in each case [10]. For example, although the bottom-up strategies are successful in controlling sizes and morphorgies of the GQDs, the time-consuming synthetic procedures, particular organic precursors, poor solubility and dispersity of the GQDs may hinder their applications [10,15,19]. The top-down strategies, on the contrary, can achieve large-scale production of GQDs with surface functional groups via relatively simple operations [10,20], while the low-yield, sophisticated separation process, requirement of special equipment as well as the difficulty in size-controlled production are the drawbacks [5,21–23]. Up to now, it is still a demand for size-controllable and high-yield synthesis of GQDs with good solubility via simple and fast separation process. Thus it is of great interest to establish novel methods to reach the above mentioned goals.

Since the discovery of M41S [24], ordered mesoporous silicas have been widely used for diverse applications including catalysis, adsorption, drug delivery and template synthesis, due to their controllable porosity and wall thickness [25–29]. As for the template synthesis, by appropriate usage of silica templates and certain precursors, a few kinds of nanomaterials with novel structures have been created successfully, such as carbon nanotube [25], ordered mesoporous carbon [26], mesoporous platinum nanospheres [27], ordered mesoporous metal oxides (CuO, NiO, β-MnO2) [28] and even ordered mesoporous non-oxide materials (SiC, BN, WS2, MoS2) [29]. More recently, mesoporous silica materials have also been used as templates for the syntheses of photoluminescent CDs. For instance, Zong et al. demonstrated a simple bottom-up synthesis of photoluminescent CDs by using mesoporous silica microspheres as templates [30]. They showed that the silica microspheres could be served as nanoreactors, in which the formation and separation of the nanosized CDs could be realized simultaneously. Yang et al. also developed a method for the preparation of photoluminescent CDs with narrow size distribution by using copolymer pluronic P123 and ordered mesoporous silica as soft and hard templates [31]. The simultaneous employment of soft and hard templates enabled the confined formation of CDs with uniform morphologies and prevented the aggregation of the obtained CDs during carbonization at high temperature as well. However, the inevitable sacrifice of silica templates is noteworthy although the preparation of well defined CDs could be achieved by these two bottom-up approaches.

Herein, we present a high-yield preparation of GQDs via a novel top-down approach. By using a pre-made carbon replica (C/SBA-15) of SBA-15 template that containing rich graphene domains as graphitic carbon source and a HNO3 vapor as the acidic scissor, the preparation of water-soluble and well-distributed GQDs have been achieved. Meanwhile, a simple and fast separation could also be realized due to the mesoporous structure of the C/SBA-15 and the oxygen-containing groups of the obtained GQDs, without involvement of tedious centrifugal separation and destructive removal of SBA-15 template with HF or NaOH. In addition, we found that the ordered mesoporous SBA-15 template could be recycled through an annealing treatment in air atmosphere, due to its high thermal stability and chemical inertness.

The obtained GQDs exhibited an excitation-independent behavior with yellow fluorescent emission. Inspired by the external oxygen-containing groups of the GQDs, we investigated their application as fluorescent probes for Fe(III) determination in tap water based on a calibration curve of Fe(III) standards of different concentrations. The results of Fe(III) determination showed that the obtained GQDs exhibited a high selectivity towards Fe(III), with satisfying recoveries ranging from 94.3% to 106%. Besides, the quenching responses towards other metal ions such as Cu(II), Co(II), Mn(II) and Ni(II) of the obtained GQDs were also noticed when the surface states of the GQD were adjusted, implying a possible widened application of the present GQDs.

All chemicals were of analytical grade and used without further purification. Stock solutions of cations were prepared from their corresponding salts with deionized water (DI water, ⩾18.2MΩ). Low angle X-ray powder diffraction (XRD) survey was conducted on a Siemens D5005 diffractometer (Siemens, Germany). Nitrogen adsorption–desorption experiments were performed on a N2 adsorption apparatus (Quantachrome Insturments, USA). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analyses were performed by using an Al Kα source, with energy of 1486.6eV at room temperature (ca. 22°C). Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were recorded on a JEM-3010 (Japan). Atomic force microscopic characterization (AFM) was performed on a Multimode 8 (Bruker, USA). The UV–vis spectra were recorded on a Shimadzu UV-1800 spectrometer (Shimadzu, Japan). The fluorescence spectra of the GQDs solutions were recorded on an F-7000 luminescence spectrometer with an excitation slit width of 10nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of ordered mesoporous silica template (SBA-15)
ce:section.ce:para: Ordered mesoporous SBA-15 template was synthesized according to previous work [32]. The detailed procedures were as follows: 8.5g Tetraethoxysilane (TEOS), 4g P123 (EO20PO70EO20, M W =5800), 105ml H2O, and 20ml HCl (37%) were mixed together to form a homogeneous solution under stirring. The mixture was then transferred into an autoclave and heated at 110°C for 24h. The final products were collected by filtration with plenty of DI water and ethanol in sequence, dried at 60°C overnight and then calcined at 550°C for 6h to get white SBA-15 powder.

As been previously demonstrated, carbon nanotubes and tubular mesoporous carbons with graphene structures could be obtained with suitable ordered silica templates and carbon sources [25,26,33]. Based on this enlightenment, the preparation of ordered mesoporous C–SiO2 nanoreactor was performed with the modified method proposed by Lu et al. [33]. The synthesized SBA-15 was dispersed into a pre-mixed trimethylbenzene (TMB) solution of furfuryl alcohol (FA) and oxalic acid (OA), with FA as a carbon precursor and OA as a polymerization catalyst. The mesoporous C/SBA-15 was obtained by firstly pre-heating the above mixture at 50°C and 90°C for 24h, respectively. Then the composite was continuously heated at 850°C for an additional 4h at a heating rate of 5°C/min in a nitrogen flow.

A fast preparation of the GQDs was achieved by using the as-prepared C/SBA-15 composite as the starting material via a HNO3 vapor cutting method. This acid vapor cutting strategy was previously used for functionalization or purification of carbon nanotubes [34] and being introduced into a rapid preparation of GQDs in this work. As suggested by Peng et al. and Pan et al., synthesis of GQDs was expectable with the starting materials consist of small domains of sp2 hybridized carbons [13,14]. Therefore, in principle, the as-prepared C/SBA-15 containing ordered mesopores may be used as the promising raw material for preparation of GQDs with controllable sizes, due to its confined mesoporous structures and filling graphitic carbons, as proved below.

Fig. 1 illustrated the synthesis route of the GQDs. The detailed procedures were as follows: 0.25g C/SBA-15 powder was loaded on a porous SiO2 grid of a glass steamer. The glass steamer was placed into an autoclave containing 2ml concentrated HNO3. The autoclave was then moved to an oven and kept at 160°C for 5h for acid vapor cutting. After reaction, the steamer in the autoclave was transferred to a clean beaker and washed with DI water for in situ filtration. Finally, a yellow filtrate was collected and labeled as the GQDs solution. A yield of ca. 48% was calculated based on the weight of dry products obtained by heating the yellow filtrate at 200°C. After production of the GQDs, the used C/SBA-15 composite (gray powder) was collected and annealed at 550°C inair atmosphere for the regeneration of the SBA-15 template (white powder).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Structure and morphology characterization of SBA-15 and C/SBA-15

ce:section.ce:para: The micro-structures of as-prepared SBA-15 template and its carbon replica (C/SBA-15) were studied using a low-angle XRD. Their powder XRD patterns were exhibited in Fig. 2 A and B, respectively. The SBA-15 (Fig. 2A) exhibited a distinguishable three-peak pattern containing a sharp peak indexed as (100) and two weak peaks indexed as (110) and (200) reflections, indicating the two-dimensional (2D) hexagonal P6mm symmetrical structure of the ordered mesoporous silica [32]. The low-angle XRD spectrum of the C/SBA-15 (Fig. 2B) displayed a similar three-peak feature with that of silica, suggesting the 2D hexagonal order of the SBA-15 template had been kept after loading of the carbon layers. The decreased intensity of (110) and (200) diffraction peaks of the resulted C/SBA-15 could be ascribed to the carbon coating and the changed scattering behavior, as suggested by Lu et al. [33]. The regenerated SBA-15 template was also characterized by low-angle XRD. As shown in Fig. 2D, the three well-resolved characteristic peaks of the mesoporous silica at low 2θ values indicated a successful regeneration of the SBA-15 template, which could also be proved by the comparison photographs of the white SBA-15 powder after regeneration and the grey C/SBA-15 after usage (see in Fig. 2C). The XRD pattern of the regenerated SBA-15 indicated that the present SBA-15 template was reusable, due to its high thermal stability and chemical inertness in acid atmosphere, thus made this top-down preparation a more eco-friendly way.

A comparison between the porous structures of SBA-15 and its carbon replica C/SBA-15 was carried out based on the nitrogen adsorption–desorption evaluation of them. The isotherms in Fig. 3 A and B displayed similar features of type IV isotherms with pronounced hysteresis loops, indicating the existence of mesopores in these two materials [33]. The hysteresis loop at high relative pressure in the case of the SBA-15 suggested the regular mesoporous structure of this template. As for the C/SBA-15, the hysteresis loop indicated that the mesoporous structure was retained after carbon loading, which was in agreement with the low-angle XRD results shown in Fig. 2. It was also noticed that the hysteresis loop of C/SBA-15 appeared at lower relative pressure (0.4–0.6) than that of the SBA-15 (0.65–0.8). The decreased value of P/P 0 implied a decreased pore size in C/SBA-15 composite, due to a thin layer of carbon coating on the inner walls of the SBA-15 template [33]. Besides, the pore size distributions of these two samples were evaluated and compared. As shown in the insets of Fig. 3C and D, the averaged pore sizes of the ordered mesoporous SBA-15 and C/SBA-15 were around 6.5nm and 3.4nm, respectively. Fig. S1 showed the nitrogen adsorption isotherm and the size distribution of the regenerated SBA-15 template. The featured type IV isotherm and the averaged pore size of 5.8nm indicated that the mesoporous structure of the SBA-15 template could be maintained after regeneration. Based on the comparison of the averaged pore size before (6.5nm, Fig. 3C) and after regeneration, a slight shrink of the mesopores in template was found, which may explain the decreased XRD intensities of (100) and (200) peaks of the SBA-15 after regeneration. Here, we also calculated the thickness of the carbon coating layers in mesoporous C/SBA-15 composite. Since there were two heating steps (850°C, 550°C) and one acidic vapor cutting step in the preparation process, it was difficult to determine where the shrink of the pores happened. However, based on the pore sizes of original SBA-15, regenerated SBA-15, and mesoporous C/SBA-15, the thickness of the carbon coating layers could be determined in a numerical range of 1.2–1.55nm, which might be ascribed to several sheets of graphitic carbons [25]. These graphitic carbons combined with the large surface area and confined pores of the C/SBA-15 nanocomposite would offer an opportunity for the formation of GQDs with narrow size distribution.

The morphologies and microstructures of the SBA-15 template and its carbon replica were characterized by SEM and TEM techniques. SEM images of the SBA-15 template (Fig. 4 A) and the C/SBA-15 composite (Fig. 4B) revealed that the rod-like structure of silica template had been kept after carbon loading at high temperature. The TEM images shown in Fig. 4C and D proved the presence of mesoporous structures of both SBA-15 and its carbon replica, which agreed well with the results of low-angle XRD and N2 adsorb–desorb characterizations.

It was also noted that the well-ordered hexagonal order of the SBA-15 template would be preserved after carbon impregnation despite the formation of hollow carbon nanotubes inside the inner channels of the template (Fig. 4D), as suggested by Joo et al. [26]. These preserved mesoporous structures would not only provide large surface for acid vapor to react with but also confine the growing of the targeted GQDs and thus lead to a size controlled synthesis of GQDs, as proved below.

Moreover, the structure of the regenerated SBA-15 template after preparation of the GQDs had also been studied by SEM and TEM (Fig. S2). It was shown that the morphology and microstructure of the original SBA-15 template were preserved after recycle, indicating a promising reliability of the regenerated template.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Structure and morphology characterization of GQDs

ce:section.ce:para: TEM was also employed for the characterization of the obtained GQDs. As shown in Fig. 5 A, the preparation of well-dispersed GQDs with a relatively narrow size distribution ranging from 2.5nm to 5.2nm (Fig. 5C) was achieved. The averaged diameter of the obtained GQDs is ca. 3.6nm based on statistical analysis of 100 dots. The inset of Fig. 5A showed a representative high-resolution TEM image of an individual GQD with good crystallinity and a lattice spacing (0.21nm), which could be ascribed to the hexagonal lattice plane spacing of d 1100 [11,35]. The stacking mode of the graphene layers in GQDs was also investigated. Based on the fast Fourier transform (FFT) patterns shown in Fig. S3, the GQD in blue box showed a turbostratic stacking structure while the GQD in red box exhibited a clear ABAB stacking [36]. Therefore, we think that the graphene layers in present GQDs were partly ABAB stacked and partly turbostratic stacked. However, the ratio of these two modes was difficult to be determined at present. Furthermore, the one-dimensional tubular shaped mesopores in SBA-15 and the usage of furfuryl alcohol might be the key reasons for the formation of punctiform GQDs considering the observations of circular or elliptical shaped GQDs under TEM. We speculated that the formation mechanism (Fig. S4) of the GQDs might be as the follows: (a) furfuryl alcohol precursor formed a mixture containing non-graphitized carbon and cylindric sp2 carbon layers inside of the SBA-15 template. However, different from the perfect carbon tubes, these cylindric carbon were formed of incontinuous graphene layers with defects and therefore could not be unzipped longitudinally to get graphene belts. (b) HNO3 vapor cutting removed the non-graphitized carbon and turned the cylindric carbon into smaller sized graphene pieces, which were likely to contain epoxy lines due to the reported oxygen-containing-fragment mechanism, as reviewed by Li et al. [10]. (c) The smaller sized graphene pieces were prone to facture due to the existence of the epoxy lines, which were considered as the oxidation active sites for the formation of circular or elliptical GQDs [13]. The thickness of the as-prepared GQDs was further characterized by AFM after deposition of the GQDs onto a freshly exfoliated mica. A typical AFM image (Fig. 5B) of the GQDs indicated that their topographic heights were less than 2nm (Fig. 5D), suggesting the GQDs consisted of several graphene layers were successfully obtained. These given sizes consistently proved the speculated formation of the GQDs under constraint.

Structural information of the obtained GQDs was further studied by FT-IR. The FT-IR spectrum shown in Fig. 6 A demonstrated the as-prepared GQDs possessed rich oxygen-containing groups including hydroxyl and carboxyl groups, as revealed by strong characteristic peaks at 3437cm−1 for OH stretching, 1722cm−1 for CO stretching, 1246cm−1 for CO stretching and 1436cm−1 for CO deformation [37,38]. In addition, XPS measurements were performed to determine the elemental configurations of the as-prepared GQDs. As shown in Fig. 6B, the XPS survey of GQDs exhibited a weak N 1s peak around 405eV and two predominant peaks representing graphitic C 1s (284eV) and O 1s (532eV), respectively. The abundant oxygen contents on the surface of the GQD (ca. 30%) ensured its high solubility in aqueous solution. The core-level XPS spectra of C 1s and O 1s were also displayed in Fig. 6C and D, respectively. Similar to previous report, the predominant C 1s spectrum (Fig. 6C) could be deconvoluted into five sub-peaks corresponded to CCO (288.8eV), CO (288.1eV), CO (286.1), CC (284.9eV) and CC (284.1eV), indicating a variety of chemical states of carbon contents [38]. The core-level XPS spectra of O 1s exhibited two characteristic oxygen states of CO (531.5eV) and CO (532.8eV), which were consistent with previous work [39]. These assignments agreed well with the conclusion deduced from the FT-IR survey.

The synthesized GQD was further characterized by Raman spectroscopy. As shown in Fig. S5, the as-prepared GQDs exhibited a D band (1366cm−1) and a G band (1609cm−1) [15]. The ratio of the integral area of D band and G band of GQDs, indexed as I D/I G, was calculated to be 1.67, suggesting the generation of oxygen-containing groups which is similar to that found previously [37]. These observations combining with the results of FT-IR and XPS characterization would further confirm the formation of the GQDs with rich oxygen-containing groups by this developed method. Therefore, we speculated that the as-prepared GQD would possess high affinity to certain metal ions due to its surface hydroxyl and carboxyl groups [40] and therefore lead to a possible usage of this GQD in the field of metal sensing, as discussed below.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical properties and sensing performance of the GQDs
UV–vis absorption and fluorescence property of the GQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The UV–vis absorption spectrum of the GQDs was studied. One characteristic adsorption peak around 228nm and a shoulder peak at 286nm were observed in Fig. 7 A, which could be ascribed to the π→π∗ transition of aromatic sp2 domains and the n →π∗ transition of CO groups, respectively [1]. Under irradiation of a UV light (365nm), the clear GQDs solution emits yellow fluorescence, as shown in the inserted photographs in Fig. 7A. To further explore the fluorescent property of the GQD, a detailed photoluminescence (PL) study was carried out. As shown in Fig. 7B, the emission peaks of GQDs maintained at ca. 550nm despite the adjustment of the excitation wavelength (310–430nm). This uncommon excitation-independent behavior that different from most of the previously reported GQDs [15,23], may be due to the relatively uniform diameters of the obtained GQDs considering the confined synthesis occurred in the present C/SBA-15 composite [10]. As a novel type of GQDs, this excitation-independent emission may benefit its application by avoiding the auto-fluorescence phenomenon, as pointed by Guo et al. [41]. Although the excitation-independent emission was observed, the PL intensity of the obtained GQDs would remarkably changed along with the variation of excitation wavelengths, with the highest PL intensity being obtained at 370nm. Therefore, a constant excitation wavelength of 370nm was selected and employed for further study.

Currently, reliable and fast determination of Fe(III) based on fluorescence signals is of considerable importance in biotechnology and clinical diagnostics due to the good selectivity, rapid response and simple operation of fluorescence assays [11,42,43]. From the view of application, the fluorescence response of the as-prepared GQDs towards Fe(III) had also been investigated in this study. The GQDs solution was prepared by diluting 0.5ml GQDs filtrate with 1.5ml DI water. It was shown in Fig. 8 A that the PL intensity of the GQDs at 550nm was gradually quenched with the addition of Fe(III), with a good linearity versus Fe(III) concentrations in a range of 3–60μM. The correlation equation was defined as I/I 0 =0.94415−0.00971*CFe(III) (R 2 =0.97), where I and I 0 were the fluorescent intensities in the presence and absence of Fe(III), respectively (Fig. 8B). The detection limit was about 0.3μM (at a signal-to-noise ratio of 3), which was lower than the maximum tolerance (5.4μM) allowed by the U.S. Environmental Protection Agency in drinking water [44]. The results showed that the obtained GQD might be promising for the determination of Fe(III) in aqueous media.

In ordered to evaluate the selectivity of the obtained GQD, the potential interfering ions such as Ag(I), Al(III), Ca(II), Cd(II), Co(II), Fe(III), Cu(II), Pb(II), Mg(II), Mn(II), Hg(II), K(I), Na(I), Zn(II) and Ni(II) were examined under the same concentration and the results were summarized in Fig. 8C. It was found that a much lower I/I0 ratio was observed upon the addition of Fe(III), indicating a high selectivity towards Fe(III) of the obtained GQDs. As suggested by previous reports, the high selectivity of CDs could be attributed to the specific coordination between phenolic hydroxyl groups and ferric ions [40]. Therefore, the abundant oxygen-containing groups including phenolic hydroxyls of the present GQD, which were confirmed by preceding FT-IR and XPS characterizations, may also be the dominant factor for its selective tendency towards ferric ions. Besides, the carboxyl groups on its surface may also be involved in Fe(III) sensing, as suggested by Zhang et al. [40]. Based on these analyses, a coordinated Fe-GQDs analogy would also be formed in present study. This Fe-GQDs complex may facilitate the photo-induced charge transfer from GQDs to Fe(III) and thus lead to a significant fluorescence quenching, as pointed out by Huang et al. [45].

In order to get further understanding of the quenching mechanism, NaOH was used for the modification of GQDs since the surface hydroxyl groups of the obtained GQD would likely be responsible for its selectivity. It had been simulated that NaOH would react with hydroxyl groups of the graphene oxide in previous reports [46]. As expected in our experiments, the NaOH-treated GQDs showed weaker FT-IR signals of OH and CO while the CC and CH peaks became stronger compared to those of the original GQDs (Fig. S6A). The NaOH-treated GQDs were then used for detection of metal ions. It was found that the NaOH-treated GQD exhibited more noticeable quenching signals towards Cu(II), Co(II), Mn(II), Ni(II) than other metal ions in neutral solution (Fig. S6B), especially with a depressed response towards Fe(III). This phenomenon could be ascribed to (a) the degraded affinity of the GQDs towards Fe(III), due to the decreased amount of surface hydroxyl groups; and (b) the appearance of carboxylates, which possessed strong chelating tendencies towards Cu(II), Co(II), Mn(II) and Ni(II), due to the conversion of the original carboxyl groups in neutral solution. Therefore, the good selectivity of GQDs towards Fe(III) was predominantly relied on the coordination between the phenolic hydroxyl groups and ferric ions. Moreover, the photostability of the obtained GQDs was tested under a continuous irradiation of 2h with an Xe lamp (150W). As shown in Fig. 8D, the luminescence of the GQDs increased only 2.8%, indicating a good photostability of this material.

To evaluate the practical application of the obtained GQD, it was also employed for the determination of Fe(III) in real water samples. In this set of test, the tap water was collected in our laboratory and directly used as diluted solvent instead of deionized water to get the GQDs solution. It was shown in Table S1 that no detectable Fe(III) was found in tap water. A series of three measurements of 10, 30 and 40μM Fe(III) solutions resulted in satisfying recoveries of 94.3–106% and RSD of 3.4–5.2%, indicating a possible fluorescent determination of Fe(III) could be achieved by the present GQDs.

The recycling performance of the SBA-15 template had also been evaluated. By using the regenerated SBA-15 template as the nanoreactor, the GQDs were re-obtained and re-evaluated. As shown in Fig. S7, the new GQDs solution emitted a yellow fluorescence under irradiation (UV lamp, 365nm) and showed a quenched PL response towards Fe(III). Besides, the satisfying recoveries of 98.3–104% and RSD of 5.1–9.2% were obtained when the new prepared GQDs was used for the determination of Fe(III) in tap water samples (Table S2), indicating the fluorescent determination of Fe(III) could be achieved with the regenerated GQDs. These results suggested that the SBA-15 template could be recycled, though the particle size of the new obtained GQDs might be changed due to the slightly shrank mesopores of the SBA-15.

In summary, we have developed a facile and low-cost top-down method for the preparation of a novel type of fluorescent GQDs. The high-yield synthesis (48%) and convenient separation of the GQDs can be directly achieved via an acid vapor cutting strategy and in situ filtration. The obtained GQDs are uniform and well-dispersed due to the usage of SBA-15 nanoreactor. Besides, the large surface area, confined pores and abundant sp2 graphitic carbon domains of the C/SBA-15 nanocomposite play key roles in acidic exfoliation. The rich oxygen-containing groups including phenolic hydroxyl and carboxyl groups of the obtained GQD ensure its good aqueous solubility. Besides, these oxygen functional groups and the excitation-independent fluorescence of the GQD lead to a feasible determination of Fe(III) with good selectivity and satisfying recovery for real water samples. Moreover, the fluorescence of the GQD can also be quenched by Cu(II), Co(II), Mn(II), Ni(II)after its surface modification, thus may widen the application of the obtained GQDs.

The present synthesis of GQDs avoids time-consuming modification and separation in terms of improving water-solubility and dispersity of resulted probes. And also, the recyclable SBA-15 template makes the proposed determination of Fe(III) more eco-friendly in the field of metal-ion detections.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors acknowledge the financial support from the ‘One Hundred Talents Project Foundation Program’, the ‘Cross-Cooperation Program for Creative Research Teams’ and the ‘Western Light Program’ from ‘Chinese Academy of Sciences’ and ‘National Natural Science Foundation of China’ (21203244, 21473247), the ‘Young Creative Sci-Tech Talents Cultivation Project of Xinjiang Uyghur Autonomous Region (2013711012)’ as well as the ‘Xinjiang International Science and Technology Cooperation Project (20146003)’.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.carbon.2015.02.036.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: This document contains Supplementary Figs. S1–S7 and Tables S1, S2.
